metadata.title: SYNGO_CC_473
metadata.comment:
Rationale: This study provides extensive characterization of PLC beta1 localization in brain, using immuno-electron microscopy (HRP labeling and immunogold pre- and postembedding).

In CA1 pyramidal cells, PLC beta1 was predominantly detected in somatodendritic compartments, including dendritic spines (Figure 3, 4). No signal was observed in presynaptic terminals. Note that most signal is cytoplasmatic, although some membrane staining is observed. The latter might be due to activity dependent recruitment of PLC to the plasma membrane via its PH or C2 domains. In the cerebellum, PLC beta1 appears to be largely absent from the synapse (figure 8).

A subcellular fractionation for PLC beta1 is also provided (Figure 3A). This result is in agreement with the EM data, but lacks controls.

Extensive characterization of the antibody is provided, including knockout controls in western blot (Figure 1A, immunofluorescence (Figure 1B) and immuno-EM (Figure 3,4).. Experimental description: C57BL/6J mice at 2 months of age were used for PLCb1 expression
analyses. We also used PLCb1-knockout (KO) mice, which were
produced previously (Kim et al., 1997) and supplied by Professor
Hee-Sup Shin (Korea Institute of Science and Technology). PLCb1-
KO mice at 2-3 weeks of age were used as a control of immuno-
histochemistry because most PLCb1-KO mice suddenly die after
3 weeks due to epileptic seizure (Kim et al., 1997). They were treated
according to the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals
of Hokkaido University School of Medicine.
